heroTitle: How China became a global biotech power
heroSubtitle: From a generics-only industry crippled by fraud to a pipeline feeding global pharma deals
intro:
In the first quarter of 2025, China produced 46% of the drugs going into clinical trials[[cite:pharmavoice-citeline-roundup-2025]] as well as 24% of the first-in-class drug pipeline.[[cite:caixin-fic-pipeline-2025]] They lead the global CAR-T pipeline, with treatments going into trials for multiple myeloma, solid tumors like gastric cancer, and refractory autoimmune diseases like lupus.[[cite:pharma-tech-cart-china-superpower]]

And it comes at a time when top-selling Western drugs will face loss of exclusivity covering an estimated $200 billion to $230 billion in annual revenue over the next five years, requiring the industry to produce roughly 60 to 100 new blockbusters ($3B+ peak sales), approximately 2-3x its historical pace.[[cite:labiotech-patent-cliff-2025]]

This cycling of patent cliffs is to some extent part of the natural progression of large pharmaceuticals. However, the cost of the current cliff is about 3x the cost of the 2011-2016 expiration that eroded $100 billion in revenue, and is particularly concentrated at the top, with five of the top ten pharma companies facing 50%+ revenue exposure.[[cite:bcg-biopharma-trends-2026]] The percentage of drugs produced in-house at these large pharmaceutical organizations has generally been trending downwards, with 70% of the clinical-stage pipeline populated by drugs from emerging biopharma, including the Chinese ecosystem.[[cite:iqvia-global-rd-2025]]

In certain ways, the rise of Chinese biotech is just like that of any other industry—talent is lured with massive capital mobilization, reorganizations of massive bureaucratic structures have enabled China to run through clinical trials at roughly 1/3 the cost[[cite:globaldata-oct-west-coast-2025]] and 2-5x the speed of its American counterparts,[[cite:iqvia-rethinking-trial-prioritization-2024]] strategically constructed hubs in cities like Shanghai, Beijing, and Suzhou enable serendipitous encounter—“mini Silicon Valleys,” according to Keyu Jin.[[cite:jin-new-china-playbook-2023]] However, there are also brilliant moves of financial engineering—the Hong Kong stock exchange’s pre-revenue listing regime enabled 75+ Chinese biotechs to access roughly $15B of public capital in seven years.[[cite:hkex-18a-cumulative-2024]]

I’ve compiled the published records of the people, the companies, the political reforms that have shaped the Chinese biotech industry into what it is today. As with many tales, there is no beginning, but perhaps we can begin at rock bottom. Let’s say hello to our friend Mao Zedong.
